Inferior work performance is probably the biggest saboteur of profitability. So let's begin with it. Places of work can be stressful, and we all know how costly that stress can be in terms of increased health care expenses, more sick leave days, and absences from work. We may not appreciate, though, how costly that stress can be when it takes the form of what we can call ethical stress, which comes from a conflict between employees' ethical values and the ethical values of their organization. In the case of employee performance, this kind of stress can be even more harmful than other known stressors. For example, a study of customer service agents found ethical stress to generate more employee fatigue and job burnout than any other type of stress. We worried that an unethical work environment would breed this kind of stress and, in the process, produce lower job performance. To find out for sure, we conducted a national survey of workers in a variety of industries and positions. The results confirmed our fears. First, the more unethical the work climate people were in, the more poorly they reported they had been performing at their jobs. Second, the more unethical the climate, the more stress they felt at work. And third, it was that particular stress that caused their poor performance.
